source.title: Scalable continuous evolution for the generation of diverse enzyme variants encompassing promiscuous activities

gist_memory.paper_problem_memory: The paper aims to recapitulate the depth and scale of natural ortholog evolution in the laboratory to generate diverse enzyme variants with expanded promiscuous activities. The initial problem is that enzyme orthologs share primary functions but have diverse promiscuous activities due to deep, independent evolutionary histories. Classical directed evolution struggles to achieve such depth (many iterative rounds) and scale (many independent replicates) because it is manually staged. The hypothesis is that using OrthoRep, a continuous directed evolution system that autonomously hypermutates a gene of interest on an orthogonal plasmid in yeast, one can evolve many independent lineages of an enzyme under selection for its primary activity, and this will yield variants with diverse promiscuous activities. The model enzyme is Thermotoga maritima tryptophan synthase β-subunit (TmTrpB), whose primary activity is coupling indole and L-serine to form L-tryptophan. The goal is to obtain a panel of TmTrpB variants with broadened substrate scope for indole analogs, useful for biocatalysis.

section_gists.heading: Scalable continuous evolution for the generation of diverse enzyme variants encompassing promiscuous activities
section_gists.gist: The introduction establishes that natural enzyme orthologs have diverse promiscuous activities due to deep, independent evolutionary histories. Classical directed evolution cannot easily achieve the depth (many rounds) and scale (many independent experiments) needed to mimic this. OrthoRep, a continuous evolution system using an error-prone orthogonal DNA polymerase to hypermutate a gene on an orthogonal plasmid in yeast, can provide depth and scale by automating cycles of diversification and selection. The study applies OrthoRep to evolve TmTrpB in many independent replicates for >100 generations, selecting only on its primary activity (tryptophan synthesis from indole and serine). The expectation is that this will generate a diverse panel of variants with expanded promiscuous activities toward indole analogs.
process_signals: Problem: classical directed evolution lacks depth and scale to mimic natural ortholog diversification.
Hypothesis: OrthoRep-driven continuous evolution under primary activity selection can yield diverse promiscuous activities.
Approach: evolve TmTrpB in many independent replicates for >100 generations, selecting only on primary activity.
methods: OrthoRep system: orthogonal error-prone DNA polymerase hypermutates gene on orthogonal plasmid p1 in S. cerevisiae.
Continuous passaging under selection for primary activity.

section_gists.heading: Results
section_gists.gist: A selection system was established by deleting the yeast TRP5 gene and forcing reliance on TmTrpB for tryptophan synthesis from indole and serine. A positive control variant TmTriple (with three mutations for stand-alone activity) rescued growth, validating the selection. Evolution experiments were set up with wild-type TmTrpB, TmTriple, and a nonsense mutant TmTripleQ90* on OrthoRep's p1 plasmid. The nonsense mutant served as an early indicator of adaptation. Multiple independent evolution experiments were run for at least 100 generations. The resulting variants were characterized for primary activity and promiscuous activity with indole analogs. The text mentions that variants showed diverse substrate profiles, but detailed results are truncated in the provided excerpt.
process_signals: Selection design: Δtrp5 yeast strain, indole-supplemented media, TmTrpB must synthesize tryptophan.
Positive control TmTriple validated selection; required high-strength promoter, indicating room for adaptation.
Evolution setup: wt TmTrpB, TmTriple, TmTripleQ90* on p1; nonsense mutant for early adaptation signal.
Multiple independent replicates run for >100 generations.
methods: Yeast complementation assay: Δtrp5 strain, indole-dependent growth.
OrthoRep continuous evolution: passaging under selection, error-prone replication of p1.
Characterization of variants for primary and promiscuous activities (details truncated).
metrics: Growth rescue as indicator of primary activity.
Promiscuous activity with indole analogs (details truncated).
failures_or_limits: TmTriple and other variants only complemented when expressed from a high-strength promoter, suggesting substantial adaptation needed.

section_gists.heading: Discussion
section_gists.gist: The study demonstrates that OrthoRep's depth and scale can broaden promiscuous activities of TmTrpB while selecting only on primary activity. The evolved variants have high primary activity (kcat/KM ~10^5 M^-1 s^-1) and diverse secondary activities, including for substrates not accepted by benchmark TrpBs. The diversity is attributed to: (1) high primary activity elevating secondary activities; (2) independent replicates taking different mutational paths; (3) cryptic genetic variation from non-neutral drift on multicopy plasmids, allowing fitness-lowering mutations to persist and explore sequence space. The approach mimics natural ortholog evolution by combining long evolutionary histories (depth) with spatial separation (scale). Limitations include the need to test generalizability to other enzymes and the scope of promiscuous activities explored.
process_signals: High primary activity (kcat/KM ~10^5) likely elevated secondary activities.
Independent replicates led to different mutational paths and distinct secondary activities.
Multicopy plasmid allowed non-neutral drift, generating cryptic variation and broadening diversity.
Approach inspired by natural ortholog evolution: depth (long histories) and scale (independent lineages).
metrics: kcat/KM values in 10^5 M^-1 s^-1 range.
Activity on 5-CF3 indole analog, not detectable by benchmark TrpBs.
failures_or_limits: Generalizability to other enzymes not yet demonstrated.
Scope of promiscuous activities explored limited to indole analogs; non-indole nucleophiles not assayed.

section_gists.heading: Methods
section_gists.gist: Methods describe plasmid construction via Gibson assembly, yeast strains and media (SC and YPD, 30°C), yeast transformation protocol, and integration of TrpB variants onto OrthoRep's p1 plasmid. High-yield yeast minipreps were used to confirm p1 integration. Strains were transformed with error-prone TP-DNAP1 for evolution. Genomic deletion of TRP5 was done via CRISPR/Cas9. The provided text is truncated, so full details on evolution conditions, passaging, and assays are incomplete.
process_signals: Plasmid construction: Gibson assembly from various parts.
Yeast transformation: standard lithium acetate/PEG method.
p1 integration: ScaI-linearized plasmid transformed into yeast with wt p1 and p2; confirmed by miniprep and sequencing.
Evolution strains: transformed with error-prone TP-DNAP1, passaged ~40 generations to stabilize p1 copy number before experiments.
methods: High-yield yeast miniprep for p1 visualization.

global_hypotheses_about_storyline: The paper hypothesizes that continuous evolution with OrthoRep, by providing depth (many generations) and scale (many independent replicates), can mimic natural ortholog diversification and generate enzyme variants with diverse promiscuous activities even when selecting only on primary activity.

The selection system (Δtrp5 yeast, indole-supplemented media) imposes multidimensional pressure: stand-alone activity, mesophilic adaptation, and potential neutral drift, which may contribute to variant diversity.
High primary activity may elevate secondary activities, but independent mutational paths and cryptic genetic variation from multicopy plasmid drift are key to generating diverse promiscuous profiles.
uncertainties_to_resolve: What were the specific evolutionary parameters (e.g., mutation rate, passaging frequency, population sizes) used in the OrthoRep experiments?
What were the detailed results of the evolution experiments, including the number of replicates, the sequences of evolved variants, and their promiscuous activity profiles?
Were there any trade-offs between primary and promiscuous activities observed in the variants?
How was the nonsense mutant TmTripleQ90* used to monitor adaptation, and did reversion occur?
What validation methods (e.g., in vitro kinetics, HPLC-MS) were used to characterize promiscuous activities, and what were the specific results?
What are the limitations regarding the generalizability of this approach to other enzymes, and what future directions are suggested?

events.summary: OrthoRep may be better poised to generate enzyme variants with diverse promiscuous activities by providing evolutionary depth and scale.
Design of selection system: delete TRP5 in yeast and force reliance on TmTrpB for tryptophan synthesis from indole and serine.
TmTriple and other variants only complemented when expressed from a high-strength promoter, indicating substantial adaptation needed.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk covers introduction and early results, establishing problem, hypothesis, selection design, validation, and evolution setup.

events.summary: Ten populations fully adapted to grow without Trp after 90–130 generations.
Bulk sequencing of TmTrpB alleles from adapted populations revealed an average of 5.6 consensus amino acid changes per population.
The set of clonal TmTrpBs contained more sequence diversity than consensus sequences, with an average of 8.7 mutations per variant, indicating cryptic genetic variation.

chunk_events.notes: This chunk contains detailed experimental procedures and results from the continuous evolution of TmTrpB, including adaptation trajectories, sequencing analysis, validation of evolved variants, and initial in vitro characterization.

events.summary: 60 randomly chosen TmTrpBs from ten continuous evolution populations were cloned into E. coli expression vectors for in vitro characterization.
methods_or_tools: cloning into E. coli expression vectors

events.summary: The 60 TmTrpBs have extensive diversity with an average of 9.3 non-synonymous mutations per variant and 194 unique amino acid changes.
Force directed graph shows interpopulation diversity exceeded intrapopulation diversity, leveraging the scale of OrthoRep-based evolution.
Variant Tri-100-1-G uniquely accepted 5-CF₃ as a substrate, while all other variants and benchmark TrpBs showed no detectable product formation.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk contains detailed experimental characterization and analysis of evolved TmTrpB variants, including diversity metrics, activity assays, and hypotheses about mutation effects.

events.summary: The authors analyze why evolved TmTrpBs have diverse promiscuous activities despite selection only on primary activity.
The authors attribute diversity to evolutionary depth (many rounds) and scale (independent replicates).
The authors propose that cryptic genetic variation from non-neutral drift on multicopy plasmids contributes to promiscuity diversity.
The authors compare their approach to natural ortholog evolution, noting depth and scale as key similarities.
The authors acknowledge that evolved TmTrpBs have lower diversity than natural orthologs.
The authors propose future experiments to scale up evolution and vary selection schedules to better mimic natural evolution.
The authors hypothesize that continuous replicate evolution selecting only on primary activity will become a general strategy for expanding promiscuous activities.

chunk_events.notes: This chunk contains analysis and discussion of the results, explaining the observed diversity in promiscuous activities and proposing future directions. It includes explicit hypotheses, limitations, and design ideas.

events.summary: TmTrpB evolution passaging protocol: cultures were passaged 1:100 in media with decreasing tryptophan concentrations until growth without tryptophan was achieved, then passaged six additional times without tryptophan.
Growth rate assays: evolved TrpB variants were tested for growth in multiple media conditions, with OD600 measured every 30 min for 24 h, and doubling times converted to growth rates.

Enzyme characterization: TrpB variants were cloned into pET-22b(+) with C-terminal His-tag, expressed in E. coli BL21(DE3), and lysates were used for small-scale reactions with indole and serine, analyzed by UHPLC-MS.
Enzyme kinetics: kcat and KM for indole conversion were estimated via Bayesian inference using Michaelis-Menten model, with MCMC sampling in Stan.
Substrate scope screen: heat-treated lysates of variants were reacted with various indole analogs and azulene, then analyzed by HPLC-MS to determine product formation.
PLP-binding assay for Tri-100-3-F: the variant lacked characteristic yellow color, and activity was restored only with 100 μM PLP supplementation, indicating poor PLP binding.
Purified protein reactions: Tri-100-3-F and Tri-100-1-G were tested with nucleophiles and 100 μM PLP, incubated 24 h, and analyzed by HPLC-MS.

chunk_events.notes: This chunk contains detailed methods for evolution passaging, growth assays, enzyme characterization, kinetics, thermostability, substrate scope screening, and PLP-binding analysis. All events are directly supported by quotes.

events.summary: HPLC-MS analysis of samples, with azulene samples further diluted 20x to avoid UV-detector oversaturation and analyzed via UHPLC-MS.
Author contributions detail specific experimental roles: G.R. cloned constructs, set up and characterized evolution experiments, and performed yeast growth rate experiments.
E.J.W. performed the panel HPLC-MS assay and indole conversion rate measurements on TmTrpBs from variant set 2, and P.J.A. analyzed the results.

chunk_events.notes: This chunk contains mostly back matter (references, acknowledgements, author contributions, data/code availability) and a brief methods detail about HPLC-MS analysis. No new research evolution events beyond experimental roles and analysis tools are described.

evidence_bank.quote: we recognized that our recently developed continuous evolution system, OrthoRep, may be considerably better poised for this challenge

We reasoned that by deleting the TRP5 gene and forcing S. cerevisiae to rely on TmTrpB instead, cells would be pressured to evolve high stand-alone TmTrpB activity in order to produce the essential amino acid Trp in indole-supplemented media
TmTriple, along with other TrpB variants tested, only supported complementation when expressed from a high-strength promoter

All six of the 100 mL cultures, and four of the twenty 3 mL cultures fully adapted and were capable of robust growth in indole-supplemented media lacking Trp after 90–130 generations (13–20 passages) (Fig. 1b, Supplementary Table 1).
For each of the 10 fully adapted populations, we PCR-amplified and bulk-sequenced the TmTrpB alleles on the p1 plasmid.
Unsurprisingly, this set of clonal TmTrpBs contained more sequence diversity than the consensus sequences of the ten populations from which they were taken.
We cloned 60 randomly chosen TmTrpBs from the ten continuous evolution populations into E. coli expression vectors for in vitro characterization.
These 60 TmTrpBs represent extensive diversity, with an average of 9.3 (±2.8 s.d.) non-synonymous mutations per variant and a total of 194 unique amino acid changes across the set
With only one exception, all individual sequences cluster near the consensus sequence for their population, meaning that interpopulation diversity exceeded intrapopulation diversity.
the ability to accept 5-CF₃ as a substrate was unique to Tri-100-1-G: all other variants, as well as the benchmark TrpBs, showed no detectable product formation with this substrate

A partial explanation may come from the high primary activities of OrthoRep-evolved TmTrpBs, as validated by kinetic measurements showing that variants tested have $k_{cat}/K_{M}$ values for indole well in the $10^{5} M^{-1} s^{-1}$ range.

Since OrthoRep drove the evolution of TmTrpB in a continuous format for >100 generations, each resulting TmTrpB is the outcome of many rounds of evolutionary improvement and change (evolutionary depth).
We attribute this to the fact that we ran our evolution experiments in multiple independent replicates (evolutionary scale).

A third explanation for the promiscuous profile diversity of these TmTrpB variants is that each replicate evolution experiment had, embedded within it, mechanisms to generate cryptic genetic variation without strong selection on primary activity.

We believe this is because OrthoRep drove TmTrpB evolution in the context of a multicopy plasmid such that non-neutral genetic drift from high activity sequences could occur within each cell at any given point.
Our approach to TmTrpB evolution was inspired by the idea of gene orthologs in nature.
We approximated this by evolving TmTrpB through continuous rounds of evolution, mimicking long histories, and in multiple replicates, mimicking the spatial separation and independence of species.
the median divergence between pairs of variants from our experiment is 4.3% with a maximum of 8%
it should be straightforward to scale our experiments further, to hundreds or thousands of independent populations each evolving over longer periods of time.

we envision that the process of continuous replicate evolution, selecting only on primary activities of enzymes, will become a general strategy for expanding promiscuous activity ranges of enzymes as we and others extend it to new targets.
All passages for evolution were carried out as 1:100 dilutions.
Resulting cultures were then passaged six additional times into growth medium lacking Trp.

TrpB variants selected for further characterization were cloned into a pET-22b (+) vector with a C-terminal 6X His-tag and transformed into E. coli BL21(DE3) cells (Lucigen).

The MCMC software used for sampling was Stan (pystan version 2.19.0.0). The sampling was performed with four separate chains, each starting with 2000 warm-up (disregarded) steps followed by 12000 posterior sampling steps.

event_graph.paper_main_problem: Recapitulating the depth and scale of natural ortholog evolution in the laboratory to generate diverse enzyme variants with expanded promiscuous activities.

canonical_events.summary: OrthoRep continuous evolution, by providing depth and scale, can generate enzyme variants with diverse promiscuous activities when selecting only on primary activity.
Design of selection system: delete TRP5 in yeast, forcing reliance on TmTrpB for tryptophan synthesis from indole and serine.
TmTriple and other variants only complemented when expressed from a high-strength promoter, indicating substantial adaptation needed.
Continuous evolution of TmTrpB in multiple independent replicates using OrthoRep with error-prone TP-DNAP1, passaging under decreasing tryptophan until growth without tryptophan.
Ten populations fully adapted to grow without Trp after 90–130 generations.
Bulk sequencing of adapted populations revealed an average of 5.6 consensus amino acid changes per population.
Clonal TmTrpBs contained more sequence diversity than consensus sequences, with an average of 8.7 mutations per variant, indicating cryptic genetic variation.
60 randomly chosen TmTrpBs from ten populations were cloned into E. coli expression vectors for in vitro characterization.
The 60 TmTrpBs have extensive diversity with an average of 9.3 non-synonymous mutations per variant and 194 unique amino acid changes.
Force directed graph shows interpopulation diversity exceeded intrapopulation diversity, leveraging the scale of OrthoRep-based evolution.
Variant Tri-100-1-G uniquely accepted 5-CF₃ as a substrate, while all other variants and benchmark TrpBs showed no detectable product formation.

The authors attribute diversity to evolutionary depth (many rounds) and scale (independent replicates), and propose that cryptic genetic variation from non-neutral drift on multicopy plasmids contributes to promiscuity diversity.
Evolved TmTrpBs have lower diversity than natural orthologs (median divergence 4.3%, maximum 8%).
The authors hypothesize that continuous replicate evolution selecting only on primary activity will become a general strategy for expanding promiscuous activities.

edges.reason: The hypothesis leads to the design of a selection system to test it.
The selection design reveals a limitation that substantial adaptation is needed, refining the challenge.
The limitation motivates the continuous evolution experiment to achieve the needed adaptation.
The evolution experiment results in adapted populations.
Adapted populations are sequenced to analyze consensus mutations.
Clonal analysis reveals more diversity than consensus, indicating cryptic variation.
The diversity prompts cloning of variants for in vitro characterization.
Cloned variants are sequenced to quantify diversity.
Diversity analysis shows interpopulation diversity exceeds intrapopulation diversity.
Functional screening identifies a variant with unique promiscuous activity.
The observed diversity and unique activities lead to analysis of underlying mechanisms.
Despite the success, the evolved diversity is lower than natural orthologs, acknowledging a limitation.
The limitation suggests future scaling and generalization, leading to a broader hypothesis.

Bulk sequencing showing average 5.6 consensus mutations.
Clonal diversity with average 8.7 mutations, indicating cryptic variation.
Cloning 60 variants for in vitro characterization.
Extensive diversity: 9.3 mutations per variant, 194 unique changes.
Attribution of diversity to depth, scale, and cryptic genetic variation.
